        return time.time()

# Python 3.11+
"""
broker_service.py — the orchestrator.

MessageBroker is the brain. It owns the topics and the consumer groups, and it
implements the four public operations: create_topic, subscribe, publish, poll.

THE ONE SMART INSIGHT for this problem
--------------------------------------
A topic is an append-only LOG, and each consumer group is just a CURSOR (an
integer offset) into that log. Reading is "give me log[my_offset], then bump my
offset". Because the log never deletes and each group keeps its own cursor,
every group sees every message exactly once, in order, independently — there is
no per-consumer copy of the data and no fan-out queue to keep in sync.

The blocking read is a textbook condition-variable wait/notify:
  - poll() holds the topic's lock, and WHILE there is nothing new at my offset,
    it cond.wait()s — which atomically releases the lock and sleeps.
  - publish() holds the same lock, appends, then cond.notify_all() — which wakes
    every sleeping consumer to re-check.

CONCURRENCY MODEL
-----------------
The CLEAN core in this file is written as if single-threaded — there is exactly
ONE concurrency seam, the `_topic_cv(topic)` hook, which hands back the object a
poll() waits on and a publish() notifies on. In the clean core that hook just
returns the topic's own Condition, and ALL the wait/notify logic is already
real (it has to be — blocking IS the feature here). The §9 ThreadSafeMessageBroker
subclass adds a SECOND guard: a lock around the topic/group REGISTRIES so two
threads creating a topic or subscribing at the same instant can't corrupt the
dicts. The hot path (publish/poll) is already correct via the per-topic
Condition's own lock; the subclass only hardens the registry mutations.
"""

from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager
from threading import Lock

from gateways import Clock, SystemClock
from models import (
    ConsumerGroup,
    DuplicateTopicError,
    EmptyPayloadError,
    Message,
    PollTimeoutError,
    Topic,
    UnknownConsumerGroupError,
    UnknownTopicError,
)


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The single coordinator. It is the only thing that sees ALL topics and ALL
# groups, so by Information Expert it owns topic creation, subscription, and the
# publish/poll routing. The entities (Topic, ConsumerGroup) own the small bits
# of state intrinsic to them (the log, the offset); the broker owns the
# relationships between them.
# 🎙️ "The broker is the brain. It holds the topics and the groups and wires a
#     publish to the right log and a poll to the right cursor. The clever part
#     is how little it does — the log and the cursor do the real work."
class MessageBroker:
    """In-memory topic-based pub/sub broker (clean single-threaded core)."""

    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
        # WHY: dict by name for O(1) routing of a publish/poll/subscribe to the
        #      right topic. Topic creation is explicit, so an unknown name is a
        #      clear typed error rather than a silent auto-create.
        self._topics: dict[str, Topic] = {}
        # WHY: groups live here, keyed by name, so subscribe() is idempotent-ish
        #      lookups and poll() can fetch the cursor object in O(1).
        self._groups: dict[str, ConsumerGroup] = {}
        # WHY: inject the clock so message timestamps are deterministic in tests.
        #      A message is identified by (topic, offset) — the offset IS the id,
        #      so the broker mints no separate ids.
        self._clock: Clock = clock or SystemClock()

    # ── concurrency seam #1: the wait/notify object for a topic ──────────────
    # WHY: poll() must wait on, and publish() must notify, the SAME object. We
    #      funnel both through this one hook so there is a single, named place
    #      that decides "what do I block on for this topic". In the clean core
    #      it is simply the topic's own Condition. The blocking logic itself is
    #      already real — it has to be, because blocking IS the feature.
    def _topic_cv(self, topic: Topic):
        return topic.cond

    # ── concurrency seam #2: a no-op guard around REGISTRY mutations ─────────
    # WHY: in the clean single-threaded core, creating a topic or subscribing a
    #      group needs no lock — nobody else is mutating the dicts. This hook
    #      does nothing. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this to wrap a
    #      real lock around the dict mutations, so two threads can't corrupt the
    #      registry while adding a key at the same instant.
    # 🎙️ "My core has zero registry-lock code. The thread-safe version overrides
    #     this one hook with a real lock. The diff is a few lines."
    @contextmanager
    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield  # does nothing — the thread-safe subclass overrides this

    # ── public API ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def create_topic(self, name: str) -> None:
        """Create a new, empty topic. Raises if the name is already taken."""
        # WHY: validate the boundary first — empty name is a programming error,
        #      so reject it with a typed error before mutating anything.
        if not name:
            raise EmptyPayloadError("topic name must be non-empty")

        with self._registry_guard():
            # WHY: re-check existence INSIDE the guard so two concurrent
            #      create_topic calls can't both pass the check and both insert.
            if name in self._topics:
                raise DuplicateTopicError(f"topic already exists: {name}")
            self._topics[name] = Topic(name=name)

    def subscribe(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
        """Subscribe a consumer group to a topic; returns the group cursor.

        A brand-new group starts reading at offset 0 (the beginning of the log),
        so it sees every message ever published to the topic. Subscribing the
        same group to the same topic twice is safe — it keeps its existing
        offset and does not rewind.
        """
        if not group_name:
            raise EmptyPayloadError("group name must be non-empty")

        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)

        with self._registry_guard():
            # WHY: get-or-create the group inside the guard so two threads
            #      subscribing the same group name don't create two cursors.
            group = self._groups.get(group_name)

            if group is None:
                group = ConsumerGroup(name=group_name)
                self._groups[group_name] = group

            # WHY: setdefault, NOT plain assignment — re-subscribing must NOT
            #      reset a group that has already read some messages back to 0.
            #      That would make it re-read everything (duplicates).
            group.offsets.setdefault(topic.name, 0)

        return group

    def publish(self, topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message:
        """Append a message to a topic's log and wake all blocked consumers."""
        # WHY: reject an empty payload at the boundary — a None/empty publish is
        #      almost always a bug, and silently appending it would let blocked
        #      consumers wake on garbage.
        if not payload:
            raise EmptyPayloadError("cannot publish an empty payload")

        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)

        # WHY: hold the topic's condition lock for the append. This is the
        #      critical section: compute the offset and append as one atomic
        #      step so two publishers can't both grab the same offset.
        with cv:
            message = Message(
                offset=topic.next_offset(),
                topic=topic.name,
                payload=payload,
                timestamp=self._clock.now(),
            )
            topic.log.append(message)
            # WHY: notify_all (not notify) — several consumer GROUPS may be
            #      blocked on this topic, and every one of them needs to wake to
            #      check whether this new message is at ITS offset.
            cv.notify_all()

        return message

    def poll(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str, timeout: float | None = None) -> Message:
        """Read the next message for a group, BLOCKING until one is available.

        Returns the message at the group's committed offset and advances the
        offset by one (so the next poll returns the following message). If no
        message is available it blocks on the topic's condition until a publish
        wakes it. If `timeout` is given and elapses first, raises PollTimeoutError.
        """
        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
        group = self._require_group(group_name)
        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)

        with cv:
            # WHY: wait_for re-checks the predicate every time it is woken and
            #      handles spurious wakeups for free. The predicate is "there is
            #      a message at MY committed offset", i.e. the log is longer than
            #      where I've read up to. It returns False only on timeout.
            ready = cv.wait_for(
                lambda: topic.next_offset() > group.committed_offset(topic.name),
                timeout=timeout,
            )

            if not ready:
                raise PollTimeoutError(
                    f"no message for group {group_name!r} on topic {topic_name!r} "
                    f"within {timeout}s"
                )

            # WHY: read at my cursor, then bump my cursor by one — this is the
            #      "commit". Holding the lock makes read-then-commit atomic, so a
            #      message is delivered to this group exactly once even if many
            #      of this group's threads poll at the same instant.
            offset = group.committed_offset(topic.name)
            message = topic.log[offset]
            group.offsets[topic.name] = offset + 1

        return message

    # ── read-only helpers (used by main.py and tests) ────────────────────────

    def topic_size(self, topic_name: str) -> int:
        """How many messages have been published to a topic (== next offset)."""
        return self._require_topic(topic_name).next_offset()

    def committed_offset(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> int:
        """How far a group has read in a topic — its committed offset."""
        self._require_topic(topic_name)
        return self._require_group(group_name).committed_offset(topic_name)

    # ── private guards ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def _require_topic(self, topic_name: str) -> Topic:
        # WHY: one place that turns an unknown name into the typed error, so
        #      every public method validates the topic the same way.
        topic = self._topics.get(topic_name)

        if topic is None:
            raise UnknownTopicError(f"no such topic: {topic_name}")

        return topic

    def _require_group(self, group_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
        group = self._groups.get(group_name)

        if group is None:
            raise UnknownConsumerGroupError(f"no such consumer group: {group_name}")

        return group


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The §9 "second version". The hot path (publish/poll) was ALREADY thread-safe
# in the core because every append/read happens while holding the topic's
# Condition lock. What the clean core leaves unguarded is the REGISTRY: two
# threads calling create_topic or subscribe at the same instant could both
# mutate the _topics / _groups dicts and corrupt them. This subclass overrides
# the one registry hook to wrap those mutations in a real lock — nothing else
# changes.
# 🎙️ "The publish/poll path was already safe — the condition variable owns a
#     lock. The only thing left to guard is two threads adding a topic or a
#     group at the same time, so the thread-safe version locks the registry."
class ThreadSafeMessageBroker(MessageBroker):
    """Adds a registry lock so concurrent create_topic / subscribe are safe."""

    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
        super().__init__(clock)
        # WHY: a single lock for the two registry dicts. It is only ever held
        #      for a couple of dict writes, never across the blocking poll, so
        #      it can't throttle the hot path.
        self._registry_lock = Lock()

    @contextmanager
    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: the ONLY override. Wrap registry mutations so two threads can't
        #      corrupt the dicts while inserting a new key at the same instant.
        # WHY: try/finally via the `with` statement guarantees the lock is
        #      released even if the body raises a DuplicateTopicError.
        with self._registry_lock:
            yield

"""
main.py — a narrated demo of the message broker.

Run it with:  python main.py
It walks 5 cases and prints "Case N ... -> OK" for each. A green run means the
broker behaves correctly on the happy path AND the tricky paths (independent
group offsets, blocking poll that unblocks on publish, and the typed errors).
"""

import threading
import time

from broker_service import MessageBroker, ThreadSafeMessageBroker
from gateways import Clock
from models import (
    DuplicateTopicError,
    EmptyPayloadError,
    PollTimeoutError,
    UnknownConsumerGroupError,
    UnknownTopicError,
)


class FakeClock(Clock):
    """A clock the demo controls, so message timestamps are deterministic."""

    def __init__(self, start: float = 1000.0) -> None:
        self._t = start

    def now(self) -> float:
        return self._t

    def advance(self, seconds: float) -> None:
        self._t += seconds


def case1_two_groups_independent_offsets() -> None:
    print("Case 1: two groups read the same topic independently")
    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("orders")
    broker.subscribe("billing", "orders")
    broker.subscribe("analytics", "orders")

    broker.publish("orders", "order-1")
    broker.publish("orders", "order-2")

    # billing reads both
    assert broker.poll("billing", "orders").payload == "order-1"
    assert broker.poll("billing", "orders").payload == "order-2"
    # analytics reads the SAME two from the start — independent cursor
    assert broker.poll("analytics", "orders").payload == "order-1"
    assert broker.poll("analytics", "orders").payload == "order-2"

    assert broker.committed_offset("billing", "orders") == 2
    assert broker.committed_offset("analytics", "orders") == 2
    print("  both groups saw order-1 then order-2; offsets at 2 -> OK")


def case2_blocking_poll_unblocks_on_publish() -> None:
    print("Case 2: a blocking poll wakes up when a publish arrives")
    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("events")
    broker.subscribe("worker", "events")

    received: list[str] = []

    def consume() -> None:
        # WHY: no message yet — this blocks inside cond.wait until publish wakes it.
        msg = broker.poll("worker", "events", timeout=5.0)
        received.append(msg.payload)

    t = threading.Thread(target=consume)
    t.start()
    time.sleep(0.05)  # let the consumer reach cond.wait and block
    assert received == [], "consumer should still be blocked, nothing published yet"

    broker.publish("events", "ping")
    t.join(timeout=2.0)
    assert received == ["ping"], f"expected ['ping'], got {received}"
    print("  consumer blocked, then woke on publish and read 'ping' -> OK")


def case3_multiple_topics_isolated() -> None:
    print("Case 3: multiple topics are isolated logs")
    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("topic-a")
    broker.create_topic("topic-b")
    broker.subscribe("g", "topic-a")
    broker.subscribe("g", "topic-b")

    broker.publish("topic-a", "a1")
    broker.publish("topic-b", "b1")
    broker.publish("topic-a", "a2")

    assert broker.poll("g", "topic-a").payload == "a1"
    assert broker.poll("g", "topic-a").payload == "a2"
    assert broker.poll("g", "topic-b").payload == "b1"
    assert broker.topic_size("topic-a") == 2
    assert broker.topic_size("topic-b") == 1
    print("  topic-a and topic-b kept independent logs -> OK")


def case4_error_paths() -> None:
    print("Case 4: every error path raises a specific typed error")
    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("t")
    broker.subscribe("grp", "t")

    try:
        broker.create_topic("t")
        raise AssertionError("expected DuplicateTopicError")
    except DuplicateTopicError:
        pass

    try:
        broker.publish("missing", "x")
        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownTopicError")
    except UnknownTopicError:
        pass

    try:
        broker.poll("ghost", "t", timeout=0.01)
        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownConsumerGroupError")
    except UnknownConsumerGroupError:
        pass

    try:
        broker.publish("t", "")
        raise AssertionError("expected EmptyPayloadError")
    except EmptyPayloadError:
        pass

    try:
        broker.poll("grp", "t", timeout=0.05)
        raise AssertionError("expected PollTimeoutError")
    except PollTimeoutError:
        pass

    print("  DuplicateTopic, UnknownTopic, UnknownGroup, EmptyPayload, PollTimeout -> OK")


def case5_concurrent_producers_and_groups() -> None:
    print("Case 5: N producers + M groups race; every group reads all N in order")
    broker = ThreadSafeMessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("stream")

    num_messages = 200
    group_names = ["g1", "g2", "g3"]

    for g in group_names:
        broker.subscribe(g, "stream")

    start = threading.Barrier(len(group_names) + 1)  # producers(1 driver) + M consumers
    seen: dict[str, list[str]] = {g: [] for g in group_names}

    def consume(group: str) -> None:
        start.wait()  # all consumers + producer start together
        for _ in range(num_messages):
            msg = broker.poll(group, "stream", timeout=10.0)
            seen[group].append(msg.payload)

    def produce() -> None:
        start.wait()
        for i in range(num_messages):
            broker.publish("stream", f"m-{i}")

    consumers = [threading.Thread(target=consume, args=(g,)) for g in group_names]
    producer = threading.Thread(target=produce)
    for c in consumers:
        c.start()
    producer.start()
    for c in consumers:
        c.join(timeout=15.0)
    producer.join(timeout=15.0)

    expected = [f"m-{i}" for i in range(num_messages)]
    for g in group_names:
        assert seen[g] == expected, f"group {g} read out of order or lost messages"
        assert broker.committed_offset(g, "stream") == num_messages

    print(
        f"  {len(group_names)} groups each read all {num_messages} messages in order, "
        f"offsets all at {num_messages} -> OK"
    )


def main() -> None:
    case1_two_groups_independent_offsets()
    case2_blocking_poll_unblocks_on_publish()
    case3_multiple_topics_isolated()
    case4_error_paths()
    case5_concurrent_producers_and_groups()
    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

    broker.subscribe("g2", "t")
    broker.publish("t", "a")
    broker.publish("t", "b")

    assert broker.poll("g1", "t").payload == "a"
    # g2 still sees "a" from the start — independent cursor
    assert broker.poll("g2", "t").payload == "a"
    assert broker.poll("g1", "t").payload == "b"
    assert broker.poll("g2", "t").payload == "b"


def test_poll_unknown_topic_raises(broker: MessageBroker) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(UnknownTopicError):
        broker.poll("g", "nope", timeout=0.01)


def test_poll_unknown_group_raises(broker: MessageBroker) -> None:
    broker.create_topic("t")
    with pytest.raises(UnknownConsumerGroupError):
        broker.poll("ghost", "t", timeout=0.01)


def test_poll_timeout_raises(broker: MessageBroker) -> None:
    # boundary: nothing published, finite timeout -> typed timeout error.
    broker.create_topic("t")
    broker.subscribe("g", "t")
    with pytest.raises(PollTimeoutError):
        broker.poll("g", "t", timeout=0.05)


def test_poll_blocks_then_unblocks_on_publish(broker: MessageBroker) -> None:
    broker.create_topic("t")
    broker.subscribe("g", "t")
    received: list[str] = []

    def consume() -> None:
        received.append(broker.poll("g", "t", timeout=5.0).payload)

    t = threading.Thread(target=consume)
    t.start()
    # consumer is blocked on cond.wait; publish should wake it
    broker.publish("t", "late")
    t.join(timeout=2.0)
    assert received == ["late"]


# ── concurrency: the headline race ──────────────────────────────────────────

def test_concurrent_producers_and_groups_no_loss_no_dup() -> None:
    """N messages published while M groups poll concurrently (Barrier-synced).

    STRICT post-state: every group reads ALL N messages exactly once, IN ORDER,
    offsets end exactly at N, nothing lost or duplicated.
    """
    broker = ThreadSafeMessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("stream")

    num_messages = 500
    group_names = [f"g{i}" for i in range(4)]

    for g in group_names:
        broker.subscribe(g, "stream")

    # all consumers + the single producer hit the start line together
    barrier = threading.Barrier(len(group_names) + 1)
    seen: dict[str, list[str]] = {g: [] for g in group_names}

    def consume(group: str) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        for _ in range(num_messages):
            msg = broker.poll(group, "stream", timeout=20.0)
            seen[group].append(msg.payload)

    def produce() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        for i in range(num_messages):
            broker.publish("stream", f"m-{i}")

    consumers = [threading.Thread(target=consume, args=(g,)) for g in group_names]
    producer = threading.Thread(target=produce)

    for c in consumers:
        c.start()
    producer.start()
    for c in consumers:
        c.join(timeout=30.0)
    producer.join(timeout=30.0)

    expected = [f"m-{i}" for i in range(num_messages)]

    # the log itself holds exactly N messages, dense and in order
    assert broker.topic_size("stream") == num_messages

    for g in group_names:
        # exactly-once: read count == N, no loss, no duplicate
        assert len(seen[g]) == num_messages, f"{g} read {len(seen[g])} messages"
        # in-order delivery: the per-group sequence equals the publish order
        assert seen[g] == expected, f"{g} read out of order"
        # uniqueness — a second guard that no payload was delivered twice
        assert len(set(seen[g])) == num_messages, f"{g} saw a duplicate"
        # committed offset ends exactly at N
        assert broker.committed_offset(g, "stream") == num_messages


def test_concurrent_same_group_threads_each_message_once() -> None:
    """Many threads of the SAME group poll a fixed log; the group consumes
    each message exactly once across its threads — no message is double-read."""
    broker = ThreadSafeMessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
    broker.create_topic("q")
    broker.subscribe("workers", "q")

    num_messages = 300
    for i in range(num_messages):
        broker.publish("q", f"job-{i}")

    num_threads = 8
    barrier = threading.Barrier(num_threads)
    collected: list[str] = []
    collected_lock = threading.Lock()

    def worker() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        while True:
            try:
                msg = broker.poll("workers", "q", timeout=0.2)
            except PollTimeoutError:
                return
            with collected_lock:
                collected.append(msg.payload)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker) for _ in range(num_threads)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join(timeout=30.0)

    # exactly-once across all threads of the group: every job consumed once
    assert len(collected) == num_messages
    assert set(collected) == {f"job-{i}" for i in range(num_messages)}
    assert broker.committed_offset("workers", "q") == num_messages
